entry.rationale: Ohio began mandatory statewide death registration in 1908. FamilySearch collection 'Ohio, Deaths, 1908-1953' (id: 1307272) is fully indexed and contains death certificates with date, county, and place of death. William H. Ferber, born December 1869 in Ohio, was last documented alive in Cincinnati city directories ~1910. His wife Emma died in 1948 and son Charles in 1967, suggesting William likely died between 1911 and ~1948. This is the highest-probability single source for the death date and place.

FamilySearch 'Ohio, Death Index, 1908-1932, 1938-1944, and 1958-2007' (id: 1949341) is a complementary index covering a wider span than the death-certificate collection. Useful if the full-certificate search yields no hit due to spelling variation or if William died outside the 1908-1953 window. Searches variant spellings (Faerber, Farber, etc.).

The 1910 US Federal Census (taken April 1910) will confirm whether William H. Ferber was alive and residing in Cincinnati at that date, and will supply his occupation and household composition. If he does not appear in 1910, it suggests death between 1900 and 1910. If he does appear, the search window opens to post-1910.

The 1920 US Federal Census will determine if William survived past April 1920 and, if so, his residence, occupation, and household. Combined with the 1910 result this narrows the death window by a decade. If Emma appears as a widow in 1920, that fixes his death before January 1920.

Ancestry holds 'Ohio, Death Certificates, 1908-1953' and the broader Ohio death index with different coverage windows than FamilySearch. If FamilySearch yields no match, Ancestry may have the certificate or index entry under a variant spelling. Ancestry also provides access to earlier Hamilton County records. Search as a fallback on the primary death certificate search.

Cincinnati city directories (Ancestry 'U.S. City Directories, 1822-1995') list residents annually. William appears in directories through 1910; systematic coverage of 1911-1935 will pinpoint the year he stops appearing, bracketing the death window to a narrow range before the death certificate search is exhausted. This is especially useful if he died during the gap years (1933-1937) not covered by the FamilySearch death index.

The Social Security Death Index (FamilySearch 'United States Social Security Death Index') covers deaths of individuals who had a Social Security number — enrollment began 1936. If William was still alive in 1936, he may have obtained an SSN and would appear in the SSDI. This narrows the death date and provides the last known state of residence.

FAN item: William's wife Emma Becker Ferber died 7 December 1948 in Dayton, Campbell County, Kentucky. Her Kentucky death certificate (Ancestry 'Kentucky, U.S., Death Records, 1852-1965') will state her marital status (widowed vs. married) and — if widowed — may record her husband's name and date/place of his death. This is an indirect but potentially precise source for William's death that cross-checks whatever the Ohio records show.

## When and Where Did William Hubert Ferber Die?

**Conclusion (Probable):** William Hubert Ferber died on **11 March 1903** in **Cincinnati, Hamilton County, Ohio**, United States.

### Evidence

**1. Ohio County Death Records (Derivative Source)**

The FamilySearch collection “Ohio, County Death Records, 1840–2001” (collection 2128172) contains an index entry for **“Wm. Ferber”** recording his death on 11 March 1903 in Cincinnati, Hamilton County, Ohio (record ark:/61903/1:1:F66M-8JZ; source S2). The index records: name Wm. Ferber (male); birth year ~1870, Cincinnati, Hamilton, Ohio; death date 11 March 1903, Cincinnati, Hamilton, Ohio; marital status Married; race White; father: Gerhard Ferber; mother: Eva Ferber.

This is a pre-mandatory-registration county death record (Ohio required statewide death registration beginning 1 January 1908). The source is a **derivative** (FamilySearch index of the original register). The original register image (linked as ark:/61903/3:1:S3HT-66TS-SCS) was sought: the page read covered Spring Grove Cemetery deaths for March 6–16, 1903 but did not show “Wm. Ferber” as a deceased person on that page — suggesting his burial was at a different Cincinnati cemetery whose death-report page was not located in this search.

**2. 1910 United States Federal Census (Corroborating Indirect Evidence)**

The 1910 US Federal Census (FS collection 1727033, record ark:/61903/1:1:ML89-JK7; source S1) records **Emma Ferber** as head of household in Cincinnati Ward 21, Hamilton County, Ohio, with marital status **Widowed**. Her household includes Gerhard Ferber (father-in-law, born Germany — consistent with tree person GCD9-9X1) and Chas H. Ferber (son, born ~1892 Ohio — consistent with tree person G7JB-Y46). William H. Ferber is absent from the household. This is indirect evidence that William died before the census enumeration date of 15 April 1910 — entirely consistent with a death on 11 March 1903.

### Identity Analysis

The death record persona “Wm. Ferber” is identified as William Hubert Ferber (G7JB-YH6) on five mutually corroborating criteria:

1. **Surname**: Ferber — exact match.
2. **Given name**: “Wm.” is the standard abbreviation for William.
3. **Birth year**: ~1870 (record) vs. December 1869 (tree) — a difference of less than four months, well within the margin expected for secondary birth information on a death certificate.
4. **Birth place**: Cincinnati, Hamilton, Ohio — exact match to the tree’s Ohio birth place.
5. **Parents**: Father “Gerhard Ferber” and mother “Eva Ferber” on the death record match tree persons GCD9-9X1 (Gerhard Ferber, born 29 Sep 1820 Germany) and G7J1-QF2 (Eva Engermann Ferber, died 1 Jan 1872 Cincinnati). The parent-name match is the strongest discriminating criterion: no other Ferber household in Cincinnati had both a Gerhard Ferber father and an Eva Ferber mother.

FamilySearch’s own person-matching system has independently attached this death record to G7JB-YH6 (matchScore 0.698, rank 1 of 12 candidates, next-best candidate at 0.286).

### Exhaustive Search Summary

| Source | Collection | Outcome |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| Ohio Deaths 1908–1953 | FS 1307272 | Negative — William died before mandatory registration |
| Ohio Death Index 1908–2007 | FS 1949341 | Negative — same coverage gap |
| Ohio County Death Records 1840–2001 | FS 2128172 | **Positive — “Wm. Ferber” d. 11 March 1903** |
| 1910 US Federal Census | FS 1727033 | Corroborating — Emma listed as widowed |

The absence from mandatory-registration collections (1908 onward) is explained by the 1903 death date: William died five years before Ohio’s mandatory system began. No conflicts were found between evidence items.

### Limitations

The conclusion is classified **probable** rather than **proved** because the underlying original death register page was not directly confirmed to contain the “Wm. Ferber” entry. The linked image page (Spring Grove Cemetery report) shows different deaths; William was likely buried at another Cincinnati cemetery whose report page was not located. Direct confirmation of the original register entry would support elevation to **proved**. The derivative FamilySearch index is well-corroborated and accepted as reliable, but original-source verification remains outstanding.

fields.narrative_markdown:
## When and Where Did William Hubert Ferber Die?

**Conclusion (Probable):** William Hubert Ferber died on **11 March 1903** in **Cincinnati, Hamilton County, Ohio**, United States.

### Evidence

**1. Ohio County Death Records (Derivative Source)**

The FamilySearch collection “Ohio, County Death Records, 1840–2001” (collection 2128172) contains an index entry for **“Wm. Ferber”** recording his death on 11 March 1903 in Cincinnati, Hamilton County, Ohio (record ark:/61903/1:1:F66M-8JZ; source S2). The index records: name Wm. Ferber (male); birth year ~1870, Cincinnati, Hamilton, Ohio; death date 11 March 1903, Cincinnati, Hamilton, Ohio; marital status Married; race White; father Gerhard Ferber; mother Eva Ferber.

This is a pre-mandatory-registration county death record (Ohio required statewide death registration beginning 1 January 1908). The source is a **derivative** (FamilySearch index of the original register). The original register image (linked as ark:/61903/3:1:S3HT-66TS-SCS) was sought: the page examined covered Spring Grove Cemetery deaths for March 6–16, 1903 but did not show “Wm. Ferber” as a deceased — suggesting his burial was at a different Cincinnati cemetery whose death-report page was not located.

**2. 1910 United States Federal Census (Corroborating Indirect Evidence)**

The 1910 US Federal Census (FS 1727033, record ark:/61903/1:1:ML89-JK7; source S1) records **Emma Ferber** as head of household in Cincinnati Ward 21, Hamilton County, Ohio, with marital status **Widowed**. Her household includes Gerhard Ferber (father-in-law, born Germany — consistent with tree person GCD9-9X1) and Chas H. Ferber (son, born ~1892 Ohio — consistent with G7JB-Y46). William H. Ferber is absent. This corroborates a death before 15 April 1910 — consistent with 11 March 1903.

**3. Cincinnati City Directories 1903–1910 (Corroborating Evidence)**

Eight Cincinnati city directory entries (1903–1910) in FamilySearch “United States City and Business Directories, ca. 1749–ca. 1990” (collection 3754697; tree sources 3BNM-TSG through 3BNM-5PW) appear in the tree as sources previously attached to William H. Ferber. The 1908 directory image (ark:/61903/3:1:3QHV-L3NF-GS41) was examined directly and reads:

> Ferber Chas A plumber h 1024 John  –Chas H cushionmkr h 1024 John  –Chas H draftsman 608 Johnston Bldg h 1560 Baymiller  –**Emma wid Wm h 1560 Baymiller**  –Gerhard h 1560 Baymiller  –Gustave A police h 1560 Baymiller

Emma is explicitly listed as **“wid Wm” (widow of William)**. No independent listing for a living William Ferber appears in the Ferber block. The same 1560 Baymiller household (Emma, Gerhard, Gustave A., Chas H.) reappears two years later in the 1910 census. All eight directory entries (1903–1910) follow the same pattern: Emma is the principal with a residence fact, “Wm” or “Wm H” is a secondary co-reference with no independent facts — the FamilySearch indexing pattern for a “wid Wm” entry. These entries corroborate that William was dead no later than 1908 and are consistent with the 11 March 1903 death date.

### Identity Analysis

The death record persona “Wm. Ferber” is identified as William Hubert Ferber (G7JB-YH6) on five mutually corroborating criteria:

1. **Surname**: Ferber — exact match.
2. **Given name**: “Wm.” is the standard abbreviation for William.
3. **Birth year**: ~1870 (record) vs. December 1869 (tree) — less than four months difference, normal for secondary birth information on a death certificate.
4. **Birth place**: Cincinnati, Hamilton, Ohio — exact match.
5. **Parents**: Father “Gerhard Ferber” and mother “Eva Ferber” match tree persons GCD9-9X1 (Gerhard Ferber, born 29 Sep 1820 Germany) and G7J1-QF2 (Eva Engermann Ferber, died 1 Jan 1872 Cincinnati). This is the strongest discriminating criterion: no other Ferber household in Cincinnati had both a Gerhard father and an Eva mother.

FamilySearch’s own person-matching system independently attached this death record to G7JB-YH6 (matchScore 0.698, rank 1 of 12 candidates; next-best scored 0.286).

### Exhaustive Search Summary

| Source | Outcome |
|--------|---------|
| Ohio Deaths 1908–1953 (FS 1307272) | Negative — death predates mandatory registration |
| Ohio Death Index 1908–2007 (FS 1949341) | Negative — same coverage gap |
| Ohio County Death Records 1840–2001 (FS 2128172) | **Positive — “Wm. Ferber” d. 11 March 1903** |
| 1910 US Federal Census (FS 1727033) | Corroborating — Emma listed as widowed |
| Cincinnati City Directories 1903–1910 (FS 3754697) | Corroborating — “Emma wid Wm” in all 8 entries; image confirmed 1908 |

No conflicts found. The absence from mandatory-registration collections is explained by the 1903 death date, five years before Ohio’s mandatory system began.

### Limitations

The conclusion is **probable** rather than **proved** because the underlying original death register page was not directly confirmed. The linked image shows Spring Grove Cemetery deaths — William was likely buried at another Cincinnati cemetery whose report page was not located. Confirmation of the original register entry would support elevation to **proved**. The derivative FamilySearch index is well-corroborated by three independent source types (death record, census, city directories) and is accepted as reliable.
